summary: I am originally from Morelia, Mexico. Growing up there was tough because it was hard to find work, which is why I decided to leave when I was 18. Morelia is where my journey began before I set out to find a better life in the United States.

Mexico was my home country where life was very difficult, especially finding a job. It was this hardship that pushed me and my cousin to leave and seek opportunities elsewhere. Even though I left Mexico, it’s always been a big part of who I am.

I came to the United States when I was 18, hoping to find a better life. The journey was scary and challenging, but I was determined to make it. After arriving, I worked as a strawberry picker in Salinas, California, and eventually got my green card under the Amnesty Act of 1986. Now I live in Lodi with my family, and I’m very grateful for the opportunities here.

When I arrived in Tijuana, I was very worried because I had heard many stories about coyotes robbing people there. Luckily, the coyote who helped me was someone I knew from back home named Hector, which made the situation less frightening. We stayed in a hotel before making the dangerous crossing into the United States.

Crossing the border was the most nerve-wracking part of my journey. I remember the Border Patrol was changing shifts at around three in the morning, and that’s when we decided to cross. We had to walk for nine hours through tough terrain without food or water, always afraid of getting caught by the Border Patrol.

My first few years in the United States were spent in Salinas, California. I worked as a strawberry picker and lived under a tree in the mountains with two friends. It was a hard life, with cold water and constant fear of being caught and sent back to Mexico.

I was fortunate to receive my green card through the Amnesty Act of 1986, which was a big turning point in my life. This act, signed by President Ronald Reagan, allowed many immigrants like me who arrived before 1986 to legalize their status. I got my green card in 1988, which finally gave me some security and peace of mind.

Ronald Reagan was the president who signed the Amnesty Act of 1986, which changed my life. Thanks to his administration’s policy, I was able to legalize my immigration status and stay in the United States. I am grateful for that opportunity because it allowed me to build a better future for my family.

Now I live in Lodi, California with my Danish wife and our three children. It’s a peaceful place where I’ve built my family and watched my kids grow up. I’m proud that all my children went to UCLA, which shows how far we’ve come since those early days in Salinas.

My wife is from Denmark, and we met about twenty years ago while I was working. Our relationship brought together two very different cultures, and I feel lucky to have her by my side. Denmark has become part of my story through her.

All three of my children went to the University of California, Los Angeles, which makes me very proud. UCLA represents the success and opportunities that my family has achieved here in the United States. It’s a symbol of how far we’ve come since those difficult early years.
